claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
circumstances: Child is 17 and not in approved education or extension period
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you some questions.

Let's start with the basics: **How many children do you have, and what are their names and ages?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd like to find out if I'm eligible for Child Benefit. 

I have one child - Alex, who is 17 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! I can see you have one child, Alex, who is 17 years old.

Since Alex is 17, I need to ask: **Is Alex currently in approved education or training?** (For example, studying at school, college, or in approved training courses)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't in any education or training at the moment. They finished school but aren't studying anywhere right now.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying. Since Alex is 17 and not in approved education or training, I need to ask a few more questions:

**Is Alex working? If so, how many hours per week does Alex work?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't working at the moment.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. This is helpful.

**Has Alex registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services?** 

(This is important because if a 16 or 17 year old leaves education or training and registers with one of these services, you can potentially continue to receive Child Benefit for up to 20 weeks.)

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex hasn't registered with any careers service or anything like that.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing that information. Just a couple more questions to complete the assessment:

**Do you live with Alex in the UK?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK and Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you. And one final question:

**Is Alex currently claiming any benefits in their own right, such as Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit?**

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex isn't claiming any benefits themselves.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all that information. Based on what you've told me, I'm afraid **you are not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex**.

Here's why:

Child Benefit normally covers children under 16. For children aged 16 or over (up to age 20), you can still receive Child Benefit, but **only if they stay in approved education or training**.

Since Alex is 17 and is not currently in any approved education or training, you don't qualify for Child Benefit for them.

**However, if Alex's situation changes** - for example, if they:
- Return to school, college, or approved training
- Register with a government-sponsored careers service (which could allow Child Benefit to continue for up to 20 weeks)

Then you may become eligible again and should contact the Child Benefit Office to update them.

Is there anything else you'd like to know about Child Benefit?

reasonings: Alex is 17 years old. According to the Child Benefit rules, you normally qualify for Child Benefit for children under 16. For children aged 16 or over, you can still qualify if they are under 20 AND they stay in approved education or training. However, Alex is not currently in any approved education or training. Alex is also not working, not claiming benefits in their own right, and has not registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services (which could have allowed Child Benefit to continue for up to 20 weeks after leaving education). Since Alex is over 16 and not in approved education or training, the claimant does not qualify for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (17 years old), who lives with them in the UK. While the claimant would normally be responsible for Alex, the child is over 16 and not in approved education or training, which is a requirement for Child Benefit eligibility for children aged 16-19. Alex has not registered with careers services and is not working or claiming benefits. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.

For children aged 16 or over, you can still qualify if they are under 20 AND they stay in approved education or training. However, Alex is not currently in any approved education or training. Alex is also not working, not claiming benefits in their own right, and has not registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed services (which could have allowed Child Benefit to continue for up to 20 weeks after leaving education). Since Alex is over 16 and not in approved education or training, the claimant does not qualify for Child Benefit for Alex.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant has one child, Alex (17 years old), who lives with them in the UK. While the claimant would normally be responsible for Alex, the child is over 16 and not in approved education or training, which is a requirement for Child Benefit eligibility for children aged 16-19. Alex has not registered with careers services and is not working or claiming benefits. Therefore, the claimant is not eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
